expanded_description: Complex geometric diagrams often contain overlapping triangles—triangles that share sides, angles, or regions without being visually distinct. Proving congruence in these configurations requires identifying shared parts, mentally separating the triangles, and tracking which parts belong to which triangle. The Reflexive Property is the primary tool for establishing congruence of shared sides and angles. This section develops strategies for analyzing overlapping triangle diagrams and constructing clear, logically sound congruence proofs.

core_concept.principle: When triangles overlap, they share geometric features. A side of one triangle may also be a side of another. An angle may belong to both triangles simultaneously. The key to proving congruence in overlapping configurations is: (1) mentally separate the two triangles, redrawing them if necessary; (2) identify all shared parts, which are automatically congruent by the Reflexive Property; (3) combine shared parts with given information to satisfy a congruence criterion; (4) prove the triangles congruent and apply CPCTC. The Reflexive Property is essential—it converts overlap into usable congruent pairs.
core_concept.explanation: Overlapping triangles are like two transparent sheets laid on top of each other. Some edges line up perfectly, some angles coincide. Your job is to see through the overlap and trace each triangle separately. A shared side is congruent to itself—Reflexive Property. A shared angle is congruent to itself—same reason. Once you identify these built-in congruences, they combine with given information to prove the triangles congruent. Redrawing the triangles separately, side by side, is the single most effective strategy for untangling overlapping diagrams.

image.alt: A diagram with two overlapping triangles shown as semi-transparent shapes. Shared side and shared angle highlighted. Next to it, the same two triangles redrawn separately with corresponding parts labeled. Reflexive Property noted on shared parts.
image.caption: Overlapping triangles: identify shared parts, use Reflexive Property, redraw separately.

sections.section_title: Identifying Shared Parts in Overlapping Triangles
sections.introduction: Overlapping triangles share sides, angles, or vertices. The first step in any overlapping triangle proof is identifying what the two triangles have in common. These shared parts are automatically congruent by the Reflexive Property and provide one of the three pairs needed for a congruence criterion.

introduction_image.alt: Several examples of overlapping triangle configurations: two triangles sharing a side, two triangles sharing an angle, two triangles where one is contained within the other. Shared parts highlighted in each.
introduction_image.caption: Common overlapping configurations: shared sides, shared angles, and contained triangles.

key_concept.definition: In overlapping triangle diagrams, a shared side is a segment that belongs to both triangles. A shared angle is an angle whose vertex and sides belong to both triangles. By the Reflexive Property of Congruence, any shared side or shared angle is congruent to itself: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AB}$ or $\angle A \cong \angle A$. These self-congruences are legitimate steps in a proof and count toward the three pairs needed for SSS, SAS, ASA, or AAS.
key_concept.context: The Reflexive Property is the most commonly used justification in overlapping triangle proofs. Without it, many configurations would lack the third pair of congruent parts needed to satisfy a congruence criterion. Recognizing when a side or angle is shared is a visual skill that improves with practice.

image.alt: Diagram with two overlapping triangles. Shared side marked with reflexive symbol. Shared angle marked similarly. Annotations explaining how to verify a part is truly shared.
image.caption: Shared parts are automatically congruent by the Reflexive Property.

process_flow.description: Identify the two triangles you intend to prove congruent. Write their vertex names in order. This clarifies which vertices belong to which triangle.
process_flow.mechanism: Decide which two triangles to target. Write them as $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DEF$ (or whatever the vertices are). If vertices repeat because the triangles share them, that is expected. Listing them explicitly forces you to identify exactly which three vertices form each triangle.
process_flow.result: Two clearly named triangles with vertices listed in order.
applications: In a quadrilateral with diagonal $\overline{BD}$, the two triangles might be $\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle CDB$.
At intersecting lines, the triangles might be $\triangle ABE$ and $\triangle CDE$, sharing vertex $E$.

image.alt: Diagram with two triangles named and vertices listed. Each triangle traced in a different color.
image.caption: Step 1: Name both triangles explicitly to clarify which vertices belong to each.

process_flow.description: Determine which parts the two triangles have in common. A shared side has the same two endpoints in both triangles. A shared angle has the same vertex and rays in both triangles.
process_flow.mechanism: Compare the vertex lists of the two triangles. If both triangles contain segment $\overline{AB}$, then $\overline{AB}$ is a shared side and $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AB}$ by the Reflexive Property. If both triangles contain $\angle ABC$ (same vertex $B$, same rays $\overrightarrow{BA}$ and $\overrightarrow{BC}$), then the angle is shared and $\angle ABC \cong \angle ABC$ by Reflexive. A segment must be exactly the same in both triangles—not a sum of smaller segments.
process_flow.result: All shared sides and angles identified and marked as congruent by the Reflexive Property.
applications: $\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle CBD$ share side $\overline{BD}$—both have $B$ and $D$ as vertices.
$\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle ADE$ share $\angle A$—both have vertex $A$ and rays in the same directions.

image.alt: Two overlapping triangles with shared side and shared angle marked and labeled 'Reflexive Property.'
image.caption: Step 2: Identify shared sides and angles—these are congruent by the Reflexive Property.

process_flow.description: Redraw the two triangles side by side with all given and shared congruences marked. This makes the correspondence visually clear and reveals which congruence criterion applies.
process_flow.mechanism: On scratch paper, draw the two triangles as separate figures. Transfer all tick marks, angle arcs, and congruence labels. The shared side appears as a pair of sides with matching reflexive marks. The shared angle appears as a pair of angles with matching arcs. This separated view makes it easy to count pairs and identify the criterion.
process_flow.result: Two clearly separated triangles with all congruent parts marked.
applications: Original diagram shows overlapping $\triangle ABE$ and $\triangle CBD$ with shared $\angle B$. Redraw them separately with all marks transferred.

image.alt: Original overlapping diagram next to two separated triangles with all parts marked.
image.caption: Step 3: Redraw the triangles separately to clarify the correspondence.

classifications.value: Several overlapping patterns appear frequently in geometry problems, each with a standard proof strategy.
characteristics: Configuration 1: Two triangles sharing a side, such as triangles formed by a diagonal of a quadrilateral. Often uses SSS or SAS.
Configuration 2: Two triangles sharing an angle, such as triangles formed by intersecting lines or an angle bisector. Often uses SAS or ASA.

Configuration 3: One triangle contained inside another, where the smaller triangle shares a vertex and parts of two sides with the larger triangle. May use Segment Addition to establish side congruences.
Configuration 4: Triangles sharing a vertex with vertical angles formed by intersecting lines. Uses the Vertical Angles Theorem plus given parts, often SAS.
classifications.behavior: Each configuration has a predictable proof strategy. Recognizing the pattern quickly tells you which parts are shared and which criterion to target.

examples.explanation:
Configuration 1 (Shared side): Quadrilateral $ABCD$ with diagonal $\overline{BD}$. $\triangle ABD$ and $\triangle CDB$ share $\overline{BD}$. If given $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$ and $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{CB}$, then SSS proves congruence.

Configuration 2 (Shared angle): Intersecting lines $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$ at $E$. $\triangle AEC$ and $\triangle BED$ share vertical angles at $E$. Combine with given segments for SAS.

Configuration 4 (Vertical angles): $\overline{AE} \cong \overline{CE}$ and $\overline{BE} \cong \overline{DE}$ with vertical angles at $E$ gives SAS.

image.alt: Three common configurations diagrammed: shared side, shared angle/vertical angles, and contained triangles. Each with shared parts highlighted and proof strategy noted.
image.caption: Common overlapping configurations and their standard proof strategies.

image.alt: Complete section overview: shared part identification, three-step process, common configurations, and redrawing strategy.
image.caption: Identifying Shared Parts in Overlapping Triangles: the Reflexive Property and redrawing strategy.

sections.introduction: Once shared parts are identified and the triangles are mentally separated, the proof proceeds like any other congruence proof. The key difference is that at least one pair of congruent parts is justified by the Reflexive Property rather than given information.

introduction_image.alt: Complete two-column proof for an overlapping triangle configuration, with the Reflexive Property step highlighted.
introduction_image.caption: Overlapping triangle proofs: the Reflexive Property provides the third pair.

key_concept.definition: The proof structure for overlapping triangles is: (1) state the given congruent parts, (2) identify the shared side or angle and state it is congruent to itself by the Reflexive Property, (3) note that the three pairs satisfy SSS, SAS, ASA, or AAS, (4) conclude the triangles are congruent, (5) apply CPCTC as needed. The Reflexive Property step is essential—it provides the third pair when only two pairs are given directly.
key_concept.context: Without the Reflexive Property, many overlapping triangle proofs would be impossible because only two pairs of parts would be available. The shared part is the key that unlocks the congruence criterion.

image.alt: Two-column proof template with the Reflexive Property step highlighted. Diagram showing which part is shared.
image.caption: Proof structure: givens → Reflexive Property → criterion → congruence → CPCTC.

worked_examples.title: Overlapping Triangle Proofs
worked_examples.problem:
Write complete two-column proofs.

1) Given: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CB}$, $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{CD}$. Prove: $\triangle ABD \cong \triangle CBD$.
2) Given: $\overline{AC}$ bisects $\angle BAD$, $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AD}$. Prove: $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle ADC$.
3) Given: $E$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$. Prove: $\triangle AEC \cong \triangle BED$.
4) Given: $\overline{PQ} \cong \overline{RQ}$, $\angle PQS \cong \angle RQS$. Prove: $\triangle PQS \cong \triangle RQS$.

step_by_step.explanation:
Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CB}$ | Given
2) $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{CD}$ | Given
3) $\overline{BD} \cong \overline{BD}$ | Reflexive Property (shared side)
4) $\triangle ABD \cong \triangle CBD$ | SSS

step_by_step.step: 2) SAS with angle bisector and shared side
step_by_step.explanation:
Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{AC}$ bisects $\angle BAD$ | Given
2) $\angle BAC \cong \angle DAC$ | Definition of Angle Bisector
3) $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{AD}$ | Given
4) $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{AC}$ | Reflexive Property (shared side)
5) $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle ADC$ | SAS

step_by_step.step: 3) SAS with midpoints and vertical angles
step_by_step.explanation:
Statements | Reasons
1) $E$ is midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ | Given
2) $\overline{AE} \cong \overline{BE}$ | Definition of Midpoint
3) $E$ is midpoint of $\overline{CD}$ | Given
4) $\overline{CE} \cong \overline{DE}$ | Definition of Midpoint
5) $\angle AEC \cong \angle BED$ | Vertical Angles Theorem
6) $\triangle AEC \cong \triangle BED$ | SAS

Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{PQ} \cong \overline{RQ}$ | Given
2) $\angle PQS \cong \angle RQS$ | Given
3) $\overline{QS} \cong \overline{QS}$ | Reflexive Property (shared side)
4) $\triangle PQS \cong \triangle RQS$ | SAS

image.alt: Four diagrams with complete proofs: (1) Shared side SSS. (2) Angle bisector SAS. (3) Midpoints and vertical angles SAS. (4) Shared side SAS.
image.caption: Overlapping triangle proofs: shared sides, angle bisectors, midpoints, and vertical angles.

worked_examples.final_answer: See step-by-step for complete two-column proofs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Reflexive Property for shared parts, combined with given information to satisfy SSS and SAS.

sections.introduction: Mastery of overlapping triangle proofs requires practice identifying shared parts, redrawing triangles, and constructing complete proofs. Work through these problems to build fluency.

worked_examples.title: Overlapping Triangles Practice
worked_examples.problem:
Write complete two-column proofs.

1) Given: $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CB}$, $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{CD}$. Prove: $\triangle ABD \cong \triangle CBD$.
2) Given: $\overline{AC}$ bisects $\angle BAD$, $\angle B \cong \angle D$. Prove: $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle ADC$.
3) Given: $E$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$. Prove: $\triangle AEC \cong \triangle BED$.
4) Given: $\overline{PQ} \cong \overline{RQ}$, $\angle PQS \cong \angle RQS$. Prove: $\triangle PQS \cong \triangle RQS$.

step_by_step.explanation:
Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CB}$ | Given
2) $\overline{AD} \cong \overline{CD}$ | Given
3) $\overline{BD} \cong \overline{BD}$ | Reflexive Property
4) $\triangle ABD \cong \triangle CBD$ | SSS

step_by_step.step: 2) AAS with angle bisector and shared side
step_by_step.explanation:
Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{AC}$ bisects $\angle BAD$ | Given
2) $\angle BAC \cong \angle DAC$ | Definition of Angle Bisector
3) $\angle B \cong \angle D$ | Given
4) $\overline{AC} \cong \overline{AC}$ | Reflexive Property
5) $\triangle ABC \cong \triangle ADC$ | AAS

step_by_step.step: 3) SAS with midpoints and vertical angles
step_by_step.explanation:
Statements | Reasons
1) $E$ is midpoint of $\overline{AB}$ | Given
2) $\overline{AE} \cong \overline{BE}$ | Definition of Midpoint
3) $E$ is midpoint of $\overline{CD}$ | Given
4) $\overline{CE} \cong \overline{DE}$ | Definition of Midpoint
5) $\angle AEC \cong \angle BED$ | Vertical Angles Theorem
6) $\triangle AEC \cong \triangle BED$ | SAS

Statements | Reasons
1) $\overline{PQ} \cong \overline{RQ}$ | Given
2) $\angle PQS \cong \angle RQS$ | Given
3) $\overline{QS} \cong \overline{QS}$ | Reflexive Property
4) $\triangle PQS \cong \triangle RQS$ | SAS

image.alt: Four diagrams with complete proofs: (1) SSS shared side. (2) AAS angle bisector. (3) SAS midpoints. (4) SAS shared side.
image.caption: Overlapping triangles practice: shared sides, angle bisectors, and midpoints.

worked_examples.final_answer: See step-by-step for complete two-column proofs.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Reflexive Property for shared parts, angle bisector definition, midpoint definition, and vertical angles in overlapping configurations.

summary: Overlapping triangle proofs require identifying shared parts that are congruent by the Reflexive Property. Key points: 1) Name both triangles explicitly to clarify which vertices belong to each. 2) Identify shared sides and shared angles—these are congruent to themselves by the Reflexive Property and count toward the three pairs needed for congruence. 3) Redraw the triangles separately to make the correspondence visually clear and to identify which criterion applies. 4) Combine shared parts with given information to satisfy SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, or HL. 5) The Reflexive Property is the most common justification in overlapping proofs; without it, many configurations would lack the third congruent pair. 6) Common overlapping configurations include triangles sharing a side (diagonal of a quadrilateral), triangles sharing an angle (intersecting lines or angle bisectors), and triangles formed by midpoints with vertical angles. 7) Once congruence is proved, CPCTC applies exactly as in non-overlapping proofs.

review_questions_image.alt: Concept map with 'Overlapping Triangles' in center. Branches: Shared Parts (sides, angles, Reflexive Property), Redrawing Strategy (separate diagrams, transfer marks), Common Configurations (shared side, shared angle, contained triangles, vertical angles), Proof Pipeline (name triangles → identify shared → redraw → criterion → CPCTC).
review_questions_image.caption: Overlapping Triangles and Proofs Review: identifying shared parts and proving congruence in complex diagrams.
